chapters.summary: Council staff calls the meeting to order, reminds attendees to silence phones and not approach the hearing table. Chair Farah N. Louis is notified the subcommittee is ready to begin.
chapters.title: Housekeeping and meeting call to order

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the subcommittee on zoning and franchises.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please place your phone on silent or vibrating mode anytime during the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do not approach the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair Lewis, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis welcomes attendees, introduces herself as Chair of the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises, and notes the presence of Councilmembers Thomas-Henry, Felder, and Salaam.
chapters.title: Chair Louis welcomes members and introduces the subcommittee

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to a meeting of the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Farrah Lewis, Chair of the Committee.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This morning we are joined by Councilmembers Thomas Henry and Simcha Felder.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Today and Salaam.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis lists the four proposals: 2950 West 24th Street in Brooklyn (CM Santosuosso's district), 63-02 Fresh Pond Road in Queens (CM Wong's district), 1160 Pugsley Avenue in the Bronx (CM Farías's district), and 1166 Bedford Avenue in Brooklyn (CM Osei's district).
chapters.title: Chair Louis outlines the four rezoning hearings on today's agenda

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Today we are holding four hearings on the following proposals.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: 2950 West 24th Street, mixed-use residential rezoning in Councilmember Santos-Oso's district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: 63-02 Fresh Pond Road, mixed-use residential rezoning in Councilmember Wong's district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: 1160 Pugsley Avenue, mixed-use residential rezoning and Councilmember Farias'district, and 1166 Bedford Avenue, mixed-use residential rezoning and Councilmember Osei's district.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis details the hybrid meeting format, registration process for remote and in-person testimony, the two-minute time limit per witness, written testimony submission via email, and decorum rules including no video recording from the witness table.
chapters.title: Chair Louis explains hybrid format and public testimony procedures

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This meeting is being held in hybrid format.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public who wish to testify may testify in person or via Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public wishing to testify remotely may register by visiting the New York City Council website at www.council.nyc.gov backslash land use to sign up.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Or for those of you here in person, please see one of the sergeant at arms to prepare and submit a speaker card.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public may also view a live stream broadcast of this meeting at the council's website.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you are.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: are called to testify before the subcommittee.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you are joining us remotely, you will remain muted until recognized by myself to speak.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you are recognized, your microphone will be unmuted.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We will limit public testimony to two minutes per witness.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you have additional testimony that you would like the subcommittee to consider, or if you have written testimony that you would like to submit instead of appearing in person, please email it to landusetestimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Written testimony may be submitted up to three days after the hearing is closed.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Please indicate the LU number and or project name in the subject line of your email.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We request the witnesses joining us remotely remain in the meeting until you are excused as council members may have questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Lastly, for everyone attending today's meeting, this is a government proceeding and decorum must be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members of the public are asked not to speak during the meeting unless you are testifying.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who are called to testify and no video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant at Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis opens the first public hearing on LUs 100–103, describing the 2950 West 24th Street proposal in Coney Island, Brooklyn. The application involves a zoning map amendment, MIH text amendment, and two special permits for a mixed-use affordable housing project with approximately 410 apartments, up to 123 permanently affordable.
chapters.title: Hearing 1 opening: 2950 West 24th Street rezoning in Coney Island

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now call open the first public hearing on LUs 100 through 103 regarding 29

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: West 24th Street, a mixed-use residential rezoning proposal in Coney Island, Brooklyn, and Councilmember Santososo's district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The proposal involves a zoning map and an MIH zoning text amendment and two special permits.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Applicant is seeking to build a mixed-use affordable housing project with approximately 410 apartments.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Mandatory inclusionary housing would be mapped as part of this application, which would require that up to 123 be permanently affordable depending on the affordability option selected.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone wishing to testify regarding this proposal remotely, if you have not already done so, you must register online by visiting the council's website at council.nyc.gov backslash land use.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For anyone with us in person, please see one of the sergeant at arms to submit a speaker card.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you would prefer to submit written testimony, you can always do so by emailing it to landusest testimony at council.nyc.gov.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now recognize count.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Stand at ease.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We're still waiting for the arrival of Councilmember Santososo.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: In the meantime, we will call the following.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis calls the applicant panel—Ron Shulman, Mark Ginsberg, Richard Lobel, Demetrios Morajanis, Van Shaw, and online participants Olga Albedaner and Matt Sloan. Council staff administers the oath.
chapters.title: Applicant panel called and sworn in for 2950 West 24th Street

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I will now call the applicant panel for this proposal, which consists of

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Ron Shulman, Mark Ginsberg, Richard Lobel, Diamotros Moraganeas,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Van Tao.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And online, we have Olga Albedaner and Matt Sloan.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Could you please, those who are here in person, could you please raise your right hand?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And state your name.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Richard Lobel.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Mark Ginsberg.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Vansal.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Demetrios Morajanis.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please keep your right hand raised.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear to tell the truth and nothing but the truth in your testimony this morning and in response to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin your testimony, which is limited to 10 minutes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You should start to conclude your presentation after eight minutes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you begin your presentation, please restate your name and organization.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Richard Lobel of Sheldon Lobel introduces the application, explaining the zoning map amendment changing R6 and R6/C1-2 to R6, R7-3, and R7-3/C2-4 districts, the MIH text amendment, and two special permits for large-scale general development and parking reduction from 167 to 143 spaces.
chapters.title: Richard Lobel presents zoning map amendment and special permits overview

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Council members, Richard Lobel of Sheldon Lobel here for the applicant in the 2950 West 24th Street rezoning with special permits.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The next slide.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: What are we seeking to do here today?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So there is the zoning map amendment aspect of the application, which changes existing R6 and R6C12 districts to R6, R73, and R73C24 districts.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: In addition to that, there will be with this a mandatory inclusionary housing text amendment, which would mandate affordability for the life of the project.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: There would also be two special permits here.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: One, a special permit for large-scale general development, which will allow for some variations in site placement to allow for a better site design, and a special permit to reduce parking at the site from 167 to 143 spaces.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: I'm going to briefly go over the land use rationale for the project, and then Mark and Van will discuss some of the architectural aspects.

chapters.summary: Lobel details the proposed 18-story, roughly 334,000-square-foot mixed-use building with 410 dwelling units (408 income-restricted via ELA program), approximately 315,000 square feet residential, 10,000 community facility, and 7,800 commercial. The new building would approximate the height of the adjacent existing 19-story building.
chapters.title: Richard Lobel describes proposed development: 410 units, 18-story building

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The next slide, the proposed development, what would result here from these actions?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Quite simply, over 400 units of affordable housing pursuant to the Deeply Affordable Program, or ELA, with HPD, as well as some community development.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: facility and commercial use.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So here this would permit an 18-story building, roughly 334,000 square feet of mixed residential community facility and commercial uses along the West 25th Street portion of the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: We'll go over the maps in a few moments.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Right now there's an existing 19-story building adjacent

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: along the West 24th Street portion of the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So the new building would approximate that height and be slightly less.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Here, this would allow for the creation of 410 dwelling units, 408 of which would be income restricted pursuant to the ELA program.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The building would consist of roughly 315,000 square feet of residential floor area, roughly 10,000 square feet of community facility, and 7,800 square feet

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: proximate of commercial floor area.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Why is this rezoning appropriate?

chapters.summary: Lobel presents the zoning map and tax map, explaining the existing non-contextual R6 zoning permits FAR up to 4.8. He notes R7-3 rezonings exist half a mile west, and the proposed rezoning fits within those parameters. The commercial overlay reflects existing uses along Mermaid Avenue and Surf Avenue.
chapters.title: Richard Lobel explains zoning context and area map rationale

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So the next slide shows the zoning map.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Hard to see, but we'll get into some more detailed maps in a moment.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: You can see the existing zoning at the site R6.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The non-contextual R6 zoning, which has been in place for decades here, would permit

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: floor area ratios upwards of 4.8 for mixed-use buildings, so close to a 5.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And at R7 and R6 zoning, the proposal allows for floor area ratios that approach that slightly below.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So this is totally appropriate in terms of the zoning context of the area.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The city has previously allowed rezonings of R7-3.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Half a mile to the west of the west.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: site further from transportation.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So the proposed rezoning here appropriate within those parameters.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The next slide is the actual tax map showing the boundaries of the zoning change.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: You can see on the northern portion of the site, a 100 foot portion plus or minus would be would be reduced.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: To allow for a commercial overlay that actually reflects those sites along Mermaid Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And then the R73 portion would be allowed on the southern portion of the site, along with a commercial overlay of 100 feet north of Surf Avenue.

chapters.summary: Lobel shows the area map, noting 17-story buildings to the east and west, a 24-story building nearby, and 16-story buildings to the north and south. The site is 0.6 miles from Stillwell Avenue station with four bus lines within two-tenths of a mile. He emphasizes the area has vacancy rates under 2% and 33% rent burden in CD 13.
chapters.title: Richard Lobel on surrounding building heights, transit access, and housing need

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The next slide is the area map, which I think tells the story well in terms of why this is an appropriate rezoning.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: We propose 18 stories at the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: You can see 17-story buildings abound both to the east and west of the site, in addition to which there's a 24-story building to the west of the site, additional 17-story buildings to the east of the site, and 16-story buildings to the north and south of the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So this is an area where this building typology is well known.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: In addition to this, you've got two wide streets servicing the block, both Mermaid Avenue at 80 feet, a wide street, and Surf Avenue at 120 feet, an extra wide street.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: We are roughly 0.6 miles from the Stillwell Avenue, Coney Island.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Stop on the train, so there is mass transit available, as well as roughly four bus lines within two-tenths of a mile of the property.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Simply put, this allows us to take area of the site that's currently used for parking and is underutilized at the

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: that and provide for 100% affordable housing in a district which has vacancy rates of less than 2%

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: and typically is a rent burden community at rent burden percentages of 33% of the residents in Community Board 13.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The following slides are pictures of the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: I would ask that we scroll through those to the plans and to the first slide that says proposed development.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And so Mark and Van can discuss the architectural aspects.

chapters.summary: Mark Ginsberg of Curtis Ginsberg Architects describes the building as creating 410 affordable units with community facility space and a supermarket along Surf Avenue. He highlights 2080 flood compliance, energy efficiency, and panelized facade system for faster construction.
chapters.title: Mark Ginsberg introduces building design and resiliency features

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: I'm Mark Ginsberg of Curtis Ginsberg Architects here with my associate, Van Shaw.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: We're excited to be here.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: This building will create, as been noted, 410 units of affordable housing, two community facility space, and a

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: commercial space along Surf Avenue that is designed to be for a supermarket.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: The building is very resilient.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: Meeting 2080 flood requirements, is energy efficient, and we think is beautiful.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

chapters.summary: Van Shaw presents the roof plan showing 20,000 square feet of landscaped area between existing and new buildings, with 70-foot spacing. The building steps down in four tiers from 17 stories to 8 stories, with adult exercise areas, kids play areas, and outdoor amenities.
chapters.title: Van Shaw presents site plan, landscape design, and building massing

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: My name is Van from Curtis Ginsburg.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Right now you're seeing on the plan is a roof plan that with a side plan that showing how the building is situated against the site.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: As you can see, there is a

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: a pretty big space in between the existing building and our building that is reserved as a 20,000 square feet of a landscape area.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: The tightest spacing in between the building of existing is 70 feet and it kind of widened up to 70 feet.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: The building kind of shape that is stepping down with four tiers with the highest 70 stories and the lowest eight stories.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: This is just a better rendering of a landscape drawing showing what kind of activity can take place in the landscape area.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: There's adult playment, adult exercise area, and a kids play area.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

chapters.summary: Van Shaw shows longitudinal elevations with stepping architecture, requests for high-end setback zoning waivers to sculpt the building while preserving units, and flood resiliency measures including raising the site six feet, moving mechanical equipment to the first floor, and landscaping the front to step up to flood elevation.
chapters.title: Van Shaw on elevations, zoning waivers, and flood resiliency measures

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: This is showing that longitudinal elevation, showing how the architecture been stepping down and articulate with the building materials and also framing the residential entries.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: So this is the view looking from the corner of Surf Avenue and 24th Street.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: The next couple of slides are additional zoning waiver we're requesting for the high-end setback.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: The main reason of the request is to basically scope the building, but also not losing the residential units.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Please, next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Yeah, so these are just technical drawings showing what are the areas that will request additional zoning waivers.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next, please.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: For the resiliency measurements, you can see in this diagram over here, we have a site that roughly need to build about six feet.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: And so that's a residential entry and so the lobby area.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: We move all the essential equipment, mechanical equipment to the first floor to address that 2080 flood adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Van Shaw
utterances.text: This is just some rendering showing what the space could be used in the public area.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: And I would add shows how we landscape the front of the building as we step up to the flood elevation.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: And with that, we're here to answer any questions.

chapters.summary: Lobel summarizes that the site is currently underutilized parking and overgrown space, surrounded by buildings of equivalent height and density, with amenities and transit supporting residential growth. He emphasizes the dire need for affordable housing and the delivery of over 400 units of low and extremely low income housing.
chapters.title: Richard Lobel summarizes project rationale and benefits

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And just to summarize, so thank you, Mark and Van.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Given the site, the location amidst buildings of equivalent height and density, the fact that much of the site which would be built upon is currently used either for parking, which is underutilized, or is

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: overgrown space, the fact that this area has amenities and can support additional residential growth, and

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: in light of the dire need for affordable housing in the area, We're happy to bring this project with literally over 400 units of low and extremely low affordable income housing to the council.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And with that, we're happy to answer any questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Santos also.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, and Councilmember Farias.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So Councilmember Santos also, if you want to provide remarks and ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: One moment, dear.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Let me just gather something.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: There you go.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Councilmember, can you hear us?

chapters.summary: Councilmember Kayla Santosuosso joins and provides opening remarks, noting the proposal for 408 permanently income-restricted units through HPD's ELA program and the existing 360 affordable units on site. She expresses support for new affordable housing but emphasizes preservation and quality of existing housing are equally important.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso opening remarks on 2950 West 24th Street

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Yes, sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I was just gathering my things over here.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone, and thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: My name is Councilmember Kayla Santosuoso, and I'm here today to discuss 2950 West 24th Street rezoning application.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: The application proposes, as you just heard, 100% affordable building with 408 permanently income-restricted units through the HPD's Extremely Low and Low Income Affordability Program, or the ELA program.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: The proposed development also contains an applicant-owned existing building on the site right now with 360 affordable units, which will remain during the course of this development.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: While I'm supportive of the production of new affordable housing on underused space, it's important to note that preservation and quality of existing affordable housing is just as important.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: So today I look forward to discussing the application and the existing building conditions more with the applicant team and hearing from any colleagues and constituents.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you to all who have and continue to participate in this public process.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And so I only have a handful of questions here.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks why the site was chosen for new development. Lobel explains the existing R6 zoning would already permit significant density but would result in mostly community facility and office space with no open area. The rezoning allows a more thoughtful building with residential focus, open space, and community amenities including a potential daycare and food store.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions applicant on site selection rationale

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And so to the applicant team, I just want to, I would love to speak to what is currently on the site and why it was chosen for new development.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Yeah, Councilmember, I'll start.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So when we began these conversations, it was apparent that

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: that the existing zoning at the site being R6 actually would permit a building with some density on the existing site without a zoning change.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So R6 permitting mixed-use floor area ratio of 4.8 would allow for a building with upwards of 300,000 square feet to be placed on the empty portion of the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: That building, however, would probably have a majority of uses.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Community facility, office space, and would also be masked in such a way as it would not allow for open area between the buildings.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Coming in with this rezoning and with a developer who has extensive experience in affordable housing allows us to do a more thoughtful building, taking the available FAR, using it

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: more for residential, which is something which is most sorely needed in the area, and also to provide for an open area between the buildings, which I think amounts to greater than 18,000 to 20,000 square feet of open area.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So it kind of is a win-win for, you know, in terms of the city, an opportunity to bring more housing, an opportunity to do some thoughtful site design, and even

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: with all that, to allow for additional community facility and commercial space, conversations with the community board were around whether or not this could potentially be allowed for a daycare space for the community facility as well as for a food store for the commercials.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So really just an opportunity to rethink the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And to provide for some sorely needed uses in the area.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I would add the

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: Plan is for the community facility uses to be resiliency hubs for the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: So in times of power outages, et cetera, there will be, excuse me, a place for people to go not only in the building, but in the existing building.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Great to hear.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks about the logic of reducing parking spaces. Lobel explains the reduction from 167 to 143 spaces (24 spaces lost), noting the environmental review found no significant impacts, bus and subway service are available, and parking studies showed low utilization. The negative declaration covered parking and traffic.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions parking reduction special permit

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I know in some of our discussions, and I think it was raised at the community board level, that there is a reduction of parking that would occur as a result of this.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I believe that's one of the special permit uses, if I'm not mistaken.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Can you explain what the logic is there and why you feel it's okay to reduce the parking?

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: The number of parking spaces?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So the parking that's currently at the site is 167 spaces, and both pursuant to our environmental review, as well as through

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: the caretaking of the site, it was determined that 167 spaces were not actually utilized at the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: In fact, oftentimes it would just be a handful of spaces that were used.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: The actual reduction pursuant to the special permit goes from 167 to 143.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So we lose 24 spaces in this area and on the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And given the location of the site, the opportunity for bus and subway service in the area of the site, as well as from our own parking studies and ones that were submitted to city planning, it became apparent that

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: As opposed to using that site for our parking spaces, it would be far better to use it in terms of the physical plant of the building.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And we do note that the Environmental Review Division did give this application a negative declaration, meaning that there were no foreseen impacts, including of parking and traffic impacts proposed for this site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So the reduction in parking here would really allow for the project to move forward, and the parking does not seem to be that severely needed.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks the current utilization rate. Demetrios Morajanis estimates about 30% utilization, roughly 48 spaces, with some occupants not paying and being contacted to relinquish spaces.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso asks about current parking lot utilization rate

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: What would you say the utilization rate of the parking lot is now with the one building on site?

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: At best, about 30%.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Maybe 48 spaces, and some of them are probably not using the spaces because they haven't paid for a while, and we're trying to contact those people to give up the spaces if they're not going to use them.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks whether the courtyard and amenities would be available to existing building tenants. Lobel confirms the open space would be shared by all residents. Morajanis adds that 75% of existing tenants voted in favor of the development via paper ballots, and describes planned amenities including a supermarket, outdoor cafe, and landscaped recreation area. Ginsberg notes playground space for children.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions open space access for existing and new tenants

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: You mentioned that this project would also entail the creation of open space that would be available.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I believe the idea is for there to be a courtyard that would be accessed and accessible by the tenants in the new building.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Can you clarify if this and any other amenities would be available to the existing tenants in the existing building?

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Sure, I'll start.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And then Mark and Demetrius can add.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So the intention would be for the open space to be utilized by tenants in the existing building as well as in the proposed building to act as a shared space and to be fully utilized by all residents of both buildings.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Is there anything to add?

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Well, what's to add is that we try to get consensus from the tenants in the existing building, of which there are 360.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We gave out paper ballots, and they returned them either by mail or bringing them to the site office that's at the site itself.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And 75% of the residents that voted, voted yes, we want this development.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They liked the idea of the shared space.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They liked the idea of the open area being turned into another sister building, affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We're going to call this building the Ocean Queen because it's going to be a beautiful building, as you can see by the photographs.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And the amenities that would be available to them would be the supermarket that would be placed on Surf Avenue

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: that would give them an opportunity to have close-by shopping.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And I think they're going to have an outdoor cafe.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We've arranged...

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: For that possibility to exist and we will do so with the tenant that's going to be taking the space at one point in time.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And sharing the space with their new neighbors in between the two buildings would be something that would be

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: enhance the community connection with others living in the area and give them recreation, a place to go to, to rest, to read, have some fun, enjoy each other's companies.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: That was all well taken by the residents of Ocean Towers.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And we look forward to that.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And the idea of the courtyard or the shared space is that that would be a landscape space.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Would that have shade, tables, chairs?

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: What are you envisioning?

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: It would have all of those amenities and more.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: It will be a very clean, desirable space, a place where tranquility will prevail, where you can bring your children, you can sit down, you can read a book.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: You know, just enjoy your life.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And that was the whole intent of that.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: I would add we're going to have playground space for children in the open space, different play equipment for them, which will also be an amenity for the residents.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks about improvements to the existing building. Morajanis reports approximately $20 million in improvements since 2013, including recent new windows, AC sleeves, and roof vents through a NYSERDA weatherization program. He describes 24-hour security, daily maintenance, and a site office for resident communication. Lobel notes 27 open violations out of 394 total DOB and ECB violations.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions existing building conditions and improvements

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And so now I want to talk a little bit about just conditions in the existing building.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I know you and I had had a conversation about that.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Just making sure, of course, that, you know, these very noble, 100% affordable buildings, which are, you know, frankly, few and far between and really helpful when they do exist.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Just making sure that also the quality of the housing is also strong.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Have you made any improvements to the existing building that you just want to note and anything you can say about, you know, upkeep in the existing building, particularly, you know,

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: after you have submitted this application and we had discussed it?

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Well, the improvements that have been made over the course of the ownership since May of 2013 in dollar amounts have been around $20 million.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: The most current improvements that were made were new windows.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: AC sleeves, roof vents being replaced.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: That was done through a weatherization program with NYSERDA.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And that was about maybe a year and a half ago.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: But we keep up everything.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: There's nothing in bad condition.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Everything is functional and in working conditions.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: The buildings are swept them up every single day.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: There's a full-time 24-hour security in that building.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: It's a well-maintained building.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We have a site office.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Our communication with the residents is constant.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We don't have any barriers between management and the residents in the building.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They come in during office hours.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They express what they need to express.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: If they need a service call, if they need to discuss something regarding their lease, or whatever it is that they need to interact with management with, we're available to them, and we have a very good relationship.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: And just to note, Councilmember, as a matter of public record, Demetrios is operating the existing building and having worked with him for years, I know he's a conscientious landlord and owner.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: Of 394 DOB and ECB violations, there are 27 open ones.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: You know, this really and in a building of this size and with, you know, the robust usage of this building with 360 units, I think he really does a fairly, you know,

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: exemplary job in terms of addressing open violations and making sure that the buildings run responsibly.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I think your audio is off.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I'm trying to save everybody from a crying baby.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso cites 2 Class A, 61 Class B, and 65 Class C open HPD violations including pest, water damage, and mold issues. Morajanis explains dismissal requests have been filed but HPD merged violations from multiple buildings into one MDR number, creating record-keeping complications. He states the physical conditions don't exist and notes 320 Section 8 apartments pass annual inspections without subsidy discontinuance.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions open HPD violations and remediation efforts

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Speaking of violations, I just want to give an opportunity for you all to address and discuss some of the open HPD violations, because there are some.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: There are, I believe, from the HPD website, two open Class A violations.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: 61 open class B violations and 65 open class C violations.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: It looks like a mix of pest issues or water damage issues with some complaints of mold.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I just want to give you the opportunity to talk about where that stands and your plans for rectifying them moving forward and making sure they don't exist at the new building.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: There are dismissal requests that have been made, and there's been a little bit of a glitch with the HPD system is how they've categorized the violations.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: There were violations on all three buildings, and we went down there.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Jimmy Guzman, my manager of 35 years that's been at Ocean Towers since 2007 that we bought it,

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: has went there looking to resolve this issue.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And what they've done is they merged everything into one building from 2950 and 2970.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They had between those two buildings around.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: 30 or 40, they put them all in 2960.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: The violations as physical conditions don't exist.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They exist of record.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We have a dismissal request to have them looked at and removed of record, and that's been an ongoing issue, a little bit complicated by the fact that HPD has taken various multiple dwelling registration numbers.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And put them into one MDR number.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And we're trying to resolve that with them.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And we're still working on it.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: They're suggesting we go see the DOB.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: I don't know what the Department of Buildings would do when this is in their court.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We're trying to resolve it.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: But I can assure you that those violations do not exist.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And another thing that would attest to that is that we have about 320 Section 8 apartments that are constantly being inspected.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Every year they get inspected for quality.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: housing standards to make sure that the quality in those apartments are being kept up and the landlord is still entitled to get the subsidy payment from the various agencies that are providing it.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Well, we haven't failed.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We haven't had any discontinuance in the subsidy of a single apartment.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: In years.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: That means whatever they find, we take care of within the time that's allocated to do so.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: So that building is constantly being looked at by the agencies, and we're constantly doing work all the time.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Any conditions that come up, and there's things that would always happen.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Nothing stands still.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Nothing is in a vacuum.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Things happen.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: The thing is, we take care of those things.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We remediate them.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We take care of our problems.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: So we don't have any suspended vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: The building's in good shape.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And so, Mr.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks about a 94-year-old tenant reportedly in L&T court. Morajanis says they searched but couldn't find the person, and would never evict an elderly tenant. Santosuosso then asks about approximately 100 eviction filings in 2025. Morajanis explains L&T court is used as a collection tool, not for displacement, and most tenants remain after obtaining payment stipulations or agency assistance.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions tenant remediation and eviction proceedings

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Morgan, if there were existing tenants that had conditions that they wanted to be rectified and my office was made aware of them,

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I'm confident that you would be able to respond and remediate those issues, right?

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Of course, Kelly.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And you had mentioned the last time we spoke that there was an elderly person, 94 years old, that was in L&T court.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: You know, we looked up the apartment that you gave us, and we combed the building for the name that you gave us.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We couldn't find that person.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: But I had assured you if there was someone like that, firstly, being elderly, you're a protected class.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We would not take a 94-year-old person and evict them.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: That would not happen.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And I told you, if it came to the point that they needed to pay, I would pay their rent.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: It just won't happen.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: It won't happen.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: People don't get evicted in our building.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We don't evict people with families.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Usually what happens if they're on legal action for nonpayment, they'll end up leaving on their own.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Evictions are rare.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: That being said, there were...

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: There were eviction proceedings filed last year.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Is the idea, I mean, there were a fair amount of them, I think.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I think they were somewhere close to 100.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Well, the filing of legal actions is a collection tool.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: When people fall behind on their rent because they somehow don't pay them for whatever the reason is, we take them to L&T court and we get court stipulations to have them pay.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And we wait for the intervention of an agency to come and help them, give them a one-shot deal from the Department of Social Services or other social agencies that may intervene and pay their rent.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: So they won't have this burden of owing rent and having these notices.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We need to collect rent because if we don't, then we can't service the building properly.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: So that's something that we have to do.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: We use L&T Court as a collection tool, not as a way.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: To displace tenants.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And so it's your testimony that, you know, these evictions that were filed last year, which would have been 2025, were largely

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: essentially, you know, legal tools used in order to collect on potentially vouchers or one-shot deals, et cetera, and that most of these tenants actually remained in the building.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council member, if you could wrap up your questions.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I just have one more question.

chapters.summary: Santosuosso asks whether the applicant intends to engage 32BJ for building services in the new development. Morajanis confirms ongoing conversations with 32BJ representative John Santos, says they are finalizing an agreement, and notes 32BJ promised to support the project at the hearing.
chapters.title: CM Santosuosso questions 32BJ union engagement for building services

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: The other thing that we discussed most recently was, I believe 32BJ is in your existing building on site.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Is it your intent to engage with 32BJ again for building services on this proposed development?

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Well, Kayla, they told me that they would be here supporting us today.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: I was speaking to John Santos, and he agreed.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And we're working on the agreement.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: My attorney was not available yesterday.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: He had personal matters to attend to, and he's working with their counsel.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: But we're all set to sign up with them.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: Just a couple of things in the agreement have to be straightened out to make it equitable to both parties.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And they promised me their support.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: And I don't know if they're here or not.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: If they are, they should just announce themselves.

utterances.speaker: Demetrios Morajanis
utterances.text: because they told me that they would be at this meeting to support the new building that's going to go up if everyone approves of it.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: So I'll wrap up, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Lavelle.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Morjanis, for being here.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And, you know, I look forward to further dialogue about the building between now and when the council, you know, actually votes on this project.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I look forward to continuing the discussion.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Should there be any tenant issues that get raised, we can discuss them further and remediate them if so.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: And I look forward to continuing the conversation with 32BJ, although.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I do believe they are virtually attending, which I was just made aware of.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: So thank you for this.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Look forward to discussing this further.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks about measures to address negative impacts on current residents during construction. Ginsberg describes an off-site assembled panelized facade system for faster enclosure and reduced noise, drilled piles to minimize noise, and pump-and-cast construction. Lobel notes a restrictive declaration for construction measures will be required on the property title prior to any work.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions construction impact mitigation for existing residents

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions before we dismiss the panel.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The first question, what measures are you proposing to address potential negative impacts on the current residents from the proposed construction?

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: So one of the things we're doing is the facade will be an off-site assembled panelized system.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: So the building will be enclosed faster, which will cut down on noise to the adjacent existing residents.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: And also the building will just be built.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: Faster and more of the work is happening off-site so there'll be less disruption on-site

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: so that's a major part of it we will the piles are largely drilled so which will minimize the noise there still will be noise I'm not gonna

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: Say there won't be, but that minimizes it.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: And it's a port and place building, so there'll be a crane, but there won't be large plank or concrete elements that are being hoisted.

utterances.speaker: Mark Ginsberg
utterances.text: It'll be pumped up and cast on site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: I'd also just note that the project, as it proceeded through environmental review at city planning, there was a requirement for a restrictive declaration to be entered into with regards to construction measures at the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So that would be entered into the into title for the property and would be required prior to proceeding to, you know, working on on any jobs on the site.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So there's.

utterances.speaker: Richard Lobel
utterances.text: So there's that is going to be in place.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks about the status of HPD conversations and the L term sheet. chapters.summary: Nikki Bridko, a resident of Ocean Towers, testifies that she and neighbors were not allowed to vote on the building. She calls the project a 'wolf in sheep's clothing,' alleges the landlord has let the existing building disintegrate, claims the new building will eliminate sunlight and destroy green space, and notes the local community board voted 29-1 against the proposal. She cites over half a million dollars in city fines against the landlord.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Nikki Bridko, Ocean Towers resident, opposes project

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nikki Bridko

utterances.text: that will eliminate our access to sunlight, and it will rob us of fresh airflow, and it will destroy a beautiful green space with hundreds of trees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nikki Bridko

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nikki Bridko

chapters.summary: Marissa Solomon testifies that the project will cause displacement of poor and working-class Coney Island residents. She states the developer wants to build over a park, not a parking lot, and cites 345 HPD complaints and 128 violations over two years. She argues the city should not reward developers with neglect records with ELA program loans covering up to 90% of construction costs.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Marissa Solomon opposes project, cites displacement concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, hi.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: Everyone in New York knows we need to build more affordable housing in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: But in order to achieve this goal, it is crucial that the City Council approve the right building projects that will actually deliver more affordable housing, not projects that just try to wear the label of affordable by actually causing more displacement of working class New York residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: The proposed West 24th Street rezoning to allow the developer to build a new building over what is now supposed to be a park for the current residents of his existing building will cause exactly that, the displacement of poor and working class people in Coney Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: It is crucial that the City Council examine the full details of this project and the full record of this developer and understand that the current building was once part of the Mitchell-Lama Affordable Housing Program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: The building left Mitchell-Lama and the current owner closed off access for residents to the park that he now wants to build over.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: He's not trying to build over a parking lot, but a park, one of the few open green spaces in Coney Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: The developer also has a long record of violations in his current building, including HPD receiving 345 complaints and issuing 128 violations over just the past two years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: And if you look at the developer's other buildings, there's a long pattern of open violations and the filing of many eviction attempts against his tenants, very few of which were approved, demonstrating his strategies for displacing existing residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: And then we wonder why the rent is so high in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: The city will never

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: ever be able to achieve its goals to build more affordable housing if the city rewards developers with this kind of pattern of neglect of their existing properties with loans of up to 90% of the construction costs for the new building under the city's ELA program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: People in Coney Island can't afford expensive land use attorneys to fight for them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: They rely on their elected officials to look out for their interests.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: It's crucial that the City Council listen to the people in Coney Island and our elected representative on this issue, both in order to truly achieve the city's affordable housing goals without displacing existing residents and to preserve other council members'ability to deliver the real

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: affordable housing their constituents demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Marissa Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

The R6A district under City of Yes permits FAR 3.9 with 25-30% affordable under MIH.
