utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: When you begin your presentation, please restate your name and the name of your organization.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You all may begin.

chapters.summary: Andrew Albrecht of Hershen Singer and Epstein introduces the two actions: a zoning map amendment from R6A/C2-4 to R7X/C2-4 with an MIH area. The actions would facilitate 144 affordable senior apartments with one super unit, the refreshment of Masjid Khalifa and its school, and new retail space.
chapters.title: Andrew Albrecht presents 1166 Bedford Avenue project overview

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair, Council Members.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: My name is Andrew Albrecht with Hershen Singer and Epstein, applicant representative.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: We're here before you today with two actions, a zoning text amendment and a zoning map amendment, R6A-C24 to R7X-C24, along with an MIH area.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: And these actions would facilitate the creation, actually next slide please, of 144 affordable senior apartments with one superintendent's unit.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: It would facilitate the refreshment of the Masjid Khalifa and its attendant school and new retail space.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Through a presentation this morning, we'll tell you about who we are, how we came up with this proposal, how it fits our needs and the needs of the community.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: community and how it fits in with the sensible land use paradigm.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Then I'll pass it off to Imam Rashid to tell you a little bit about the history of the masjid and what informed this action.

chapters.summary: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid recounts the history of Masjid Abdul-Masjid Khalifa, opened in 1962 by El-Hajj Malik Shabazz (Malcolm X). He describes the community's transformation from blight, the 50-plus years of community work including a pre-K through 8th grade school, and the decision to find a development partner to maintain the institution for another 50-100 years given the aging 100-year-old structure.
chapters.title: Imam Rodney Rashid recounts Masjid Khalifa history from Malcolm X era

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chairman, Chairwoman Lewis, committee members.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: My name is Imam Rodney Adib Rashid.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And next slide.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Continue.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Continue.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Right there.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: We'll start there.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Our history.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: The doors of that masjid, Masjid Abdul-Masjid Khalifa, was opened in 1962 by El-Hajj Malik Shabazz.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: We all know him as Minister Malcolm.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And if you look at this picture, I just want to share with you real briefly that what it doesn't show is that before

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Minister Malcolm and their community opened those doors, that community was basically in blight because there was a place called the Asuna Ballroom, which closed down.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: The Pillar Factory closed down.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: The stores closed down.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And less desirable elements was occupying that space.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: But as you can see, Malcolm is engaging the press and community members.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And, of course, we have Imam Muhammad in that picture as well.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Now, that slide represents the community that's there at the same location at least 50 years later, occupying that same space with festivities, a school from

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: pre-K to eighth grade, community outreach, and things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Now, that's the work we've been doing at least 50 plus years.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: The point why we're here today is because maintenance for that structure is over 100 years old.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: It's wearing on the community, and we have to make a decision.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Our decision was to stay there another 50 to 100 years, God willing.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: But we had to find a partner.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And that's where Khalil and companies come in.

chapters.summary: Pierre Downing of Khalil Companies describes the firm as a mission-oriented, five-year-old developer with 15 years of initial work, $2 billion in city business across 5,000 housing units. The partnership aims to provide senior affordable housing, deliver a state-of-the-art masjid and school, with the masjid as joint venture partner and owner-operator of the school.
chapters.title: Pierre Downing presents Khalil Companies background and project vision

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And I'm going to pass it on to Pierre Downing.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Madam Chair and Council.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: My name is Pierre Downing, just testifying on behalf of the Masjid Khalifa Project.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So as Imam put it, this is a partnership about recognizing community, making sure that the

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: seniors and aging population of Bed-Stuy can age gracefully, remain in our community without being pushed out.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: This has been a labor of love for the past five years or so now since we first crossed paths through just our various relationships because we're known as a mission-oriented and aligned

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: for-profit developer and 100% of our business essentially has been done in partnership with nonprofits and other for-profits and city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: But a little bit about Kal-El.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: We are a five-year-old firm, gone gone five years, but our initial kind of work started 15 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And to date, we've done about $2 billion in business with the city across 5,000 units of housing.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And we can't be more than excited just to be able to do this and bring history, like a refreshment of history to the site.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Next slide.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: You can go over the next one.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: One more.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And just so the overall vision for the Masjid has been through, you know, our discussions and our design team discussions to maximize the development potential of the site with provision of senior affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: approximately 145 units, inclusive of one super.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And that unit mix and massing is required here so that we're able to deliver a state-of-the-art masjid facility and school back to the community and to Masjid Khalifa.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And they are both, you know, the owner-operator of the school and they are joint venture on the real estate.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: But without this vision and without this action, then this would not be made possible.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And we can move on to the next slide.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And here you can just see a nice rendering before I hand it over to Shaniqua Henry from SLM of the proposed site on the massing of the residential structure and the masjid.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And with that, I'll turn it over to Shaniqua.

chapters.summary: Shaniqua Henry of SLM Architecture describes the firm's mission to impact 10,000 families through design reflecting community voices. The Khalifa Masjid School and Residences design honors the legacy of Malcolm X and Imam W.D. Muhammad, with a material palette paying homage to existing brickwork. The 12-story configuration, two stories below the R7X maximum, sets back from the mosque to preserve its prominence as a spiritual anchor.
chapters.title: Shaniqua Henry presents architectural design and community context

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: My name is Shaniqua Henry.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: I'm the principal of SLM Architecture PC.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: We're a New York City-based certified WMBE.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: My husband and I founded nearly two decades ago.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Our mission is to impact the lives of over 10,000 families through design that reflects the voices, histories, and identities of the communities that we serve.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Through intentional community engagement, we work to create spaces that honor local culture and foster a sense of belonging.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The Khalifa Masjid School and Residences embodies this approach.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: It continues the legacy of Masjid Khalifa and Claire Muhammad School, providing spaces that support the growth of mind, body, and spirit.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The inclusion of commercial spaces aligns with the principles of Malcolm X and Imam W.D.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Muhammad, advancing economic empowerment and self-determination.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Our design is grounded in its context.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The material palette and facade that you see articulates and pay homage to the existing buildings, brick color, and ornamentation.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: To realize this vision, we're seeking an increase in zoning to provide 144 units of senior housing.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: After studying alternatives and in close collaboration with Khalifa Mosque and Khalil Companies, we concluded that a 12-story configuration offers the most balanced response and is still two stories below the maximum building height that is permitted in an R7X district.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: 12 stories allows us to set the residential building back from the mosque, preserving the mosque's prominence as a spiritual anchor in the community.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: In conclusion, the Khalifa Masjid School and Residences represent what we as a community stand for, which is partnership, purpose, and pride.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Its design is rooted in community, honoring heritage, expanding opportunity, and building stability for generations to come.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: Next slide.

chapters.summary: Henry details the approximately 13,000-square-foot community facility including a multipurpose room, administrative offices, library, eight classrooms, and Musala space. The residential program includes 6,200 square feet of commercial space, 75,000 square feet of residential with 110 senior studios, 34 one-bedrooms, and one two-bedroom super unit, entered along Putnam Avenue.
chapters.title: Shaniqua Henry on community facility and residential program details

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: For the community facility, which is what we're looking at here, what you'll see is that the ground floor and cellar space, excuse me, are dedicated towards the community building.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: There's a multipurpose room in the cellar.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The first floor has administrative offices for the school as well as a library.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: At the second floor, there are eight classrooms as well as common area gathering spaces for students.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: And at the third floor is the Musala space.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The total community facility is approximately 13,000 square feet.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The next slide.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: The residential program, the existing residential entry is located along Putnam Avenue, where the building is recessed and commercial spaces are located at the ground floor.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: There's a total of approximately 6,000 square feet of commercial space.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: I believe it's exactly 6,200.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: There's also 75,000 square feet of residential for 144 units, 110 of which are senior studios, and 34 are one bedrooms and a two bedroom supers unit.

utterances.speaker: Shaniqua Henry
utterances.text: And I'm going to conclude.

chapters.summary: Albrecht explains the R7X district allows shaping bulk to give presence to each program section and makes HPD subsidy go further. The added density addresses Bed-Stuy's housing affordability crisis, with excellent transit access including G, A, C, shuttle, LIRR, and multiple bus routes. All nearby transit stations will be ADA accessible by end of decade per MTA. The project received unanimous approvals throughout public review.
chapters.title: Andrew Albrecht on R7X zoning rationale and transit access

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: So this whole program is made possible by the R7X district, and the district works well here for two principal reasons.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: One, it allows us to shape the bulk of the building to give presence to each section of the program.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: The greater bulk allows the HBD subsidy to go further, making it...

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: More appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Now, this district is well appropriate in this area.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: It's good from land use perspective.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: You know, the added density helps us address the housing affordability crisis in Bed-Stuy.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: There's terrific transit access, the G, the A and the C, the shuttle, the Long Island Railroad,

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Burl Spanian bus routes running right nearby, going in each cardinal direction.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: And next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: And all of these transit access locations will be ADA accessible by the end of the decade, according to the MTA.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Excuse me, substantially all.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: Ensuring that these seniors can continue to use this project.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: You know, we're on Bedford Avenue, which will be further activated as a commercial corridor by this action.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: And the community has recognized us, which we've been really proud about.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: You know, everyone in the public review so far has approved us with flying colors.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: And so I'll turn it over to Pierre to show.

utterances.speaker: Andrew Albrecht
utterances.text: So what will happen from now to building?

chapters.summary: Downing outlines the project timeline: community outreach began spring 2025 with CM Osei, committee meetings through fall 2025, CB3 unanimous approval and Borough President approval earlier this year. The team has been working with HPD for 1.5 years on financials, targeting closing summer 2027 with a two-year construction period thereafter.
chapters.title: Pierre Downing presents project timeline and HPD pipeline status

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Next slide, please.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So here you just have an overview of kind of where we are and where we're headed.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: You can see just in terms of community outreach, we began this process in spring of 25, you know, with Councilmember Chiosi, and then we kind of moved through the various, you know, committee meetings through the fall of 2025.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And then earlier this year, we met with the community board, and as Andrew indicated, got a unanimous approval, and they got a favorable outcome from the real president.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If you guys could wrap it up from here.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Oh, it's over.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: In terms of financial closing, we have been working in Tandem with HPD for the past year and a half, working through the financials.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Or the financial execution, and we are in the pipeline, presuming a positive outcome here for closing summer of next year.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So, and then we have a two-year construction project essentially after that.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And then, you know, it's back to bed sty.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks for the unit mix breakdown. Downing reports 110 studio apartments, 34 one-bedroom units, and one two-bedroom super unit, all for seniors at 60% AMI and below, with approximately 44 units set aside for formerly homeless individuals at a deeper affordability tier.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions unit mix and affordability levels

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I have a few questions about this proposal.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You are proposing a senior affordable residential unit as part of your application.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Can you provide a breakdown of the unit mix?

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So we have about 110 one-bedroom units.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: Sorry, 110 studio apartments.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: and 34 one-bedroom units and one two-bedroom unit.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: 100% of the units are set aside for families, I mean for seniors at 60% of AMI and below.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And we have a deeper affordability tier for formerly homeless, and that's approximately 44 of the 144 units.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks where the mosque and school will relocate during demolition and reconstruction. Imam Rashid reports they have secured two temporary spaces through Community Board 3 on Fulton Street, with a commitment to use them until construction is complete.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions mosque and school relocation during construction

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Where will the mosque and school relocate during demolition and reconstruction?

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So during the demo and construction phase of the project, the masjid has been working collaboratively with other faith-based organizations in the community to set up a temporary location, and I'll let the imam speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Yeah, we have, we work with the community board three on Fulton Street, and we have two spaces when we're ready to go.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: We have a promise and a commitment from them.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: We can use that until construction is over.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks about the relocation consultant for existing businesses and whether any have expressed interest in returning. Imam Rashid confirms a couple of businesses have engaged with the relocation specialist over the past two weeks. Downing adds the consultant operates in compliance with the Uniform Relocation Act due to the HPD financing.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions business relocation consultant and tenant interest

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I understand that you have hired a relocation consultant to assist with existing businesses within relocation.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Have any businesses utilized this resource and have any businesses expressed interest in returning to the new development?

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Yes, we have a couple of businesses that express interest in returning, and they have engaged over the last two weeks, and they're still figuring out what they want to do.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: But they have spoken to the relocation specialists, and that's still a work in progress.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: And just to add to that, the relocation specialist is doing things in compliance with the Uniform Relocation Act without compliance because of the...

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: The financing execution for the site.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: So those meetings are taking place.

utterances.speaker: Pierre Downing
utterances.text: All commercial tenants have access to the consultant to work collaboratively with them so that we can figure out a plan for their temporary relocation or permanent relocation if they so choose not to come back.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis asks whether the team considered building to the maximum allowable 14 stories for additional affordable units. A panelist explains they are maximizing permitted zoning FAR, and chose 12 stories to set back from the mosque and preserve its prominence as an anchor on the corner of Bedford and Madison.
chapters.title: Chair Louis questions why building doesn't reach maximum 14-story height

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And did you consider building up to the maximum allowable height of 14 stories to generate additional affordable units?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are maximizing the permitted zoning FAR, which means that we can't go any higher.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we are also, we were very considerate of the neighboring properties, as well as we went up to the 12 stories

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: in order to set back from the mosque and have them still be an anchor on the corner of Bedford and Madison.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I will now invite my colleagues to ask questions.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Elsie Encarnación asks who owns the current building and who will own it after the project. Imam Rashid confirms the masjid will remain the owner, explaining it is a partnership, not a sale. Encarnación expresses satisfaction that the community retains ownership.
chapters.title: CM Encarnación questions ownership structure of masjid post-development

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Councilmember Encanason.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Who owns the current building now?

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: The master.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: And who will own it once this goes through?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Will you remain owner of the building?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: So it's not a sale of the building.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: It's just a partnership that you guys are creating.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: That's important.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: That's why it took us so long to.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I know that's why I'm asking.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: I want to make sure you keep your building.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: It took us so long to find somebody that would go for that.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: That was my only question.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Any other colleagues who questioned?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Yes, Councilmember Salah.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Yusef Salaam offers comments of support, noting he has seen similar projects throughout the country and is excited about this one in New York. He emphasizes the need for Muslim, Jewish, and Christian communities to be anchored properly and calls the project a much-needed example for the city.
chapters.title: CM Salaam expresses support for faith-based community development project

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yeah, I have a comment.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I've seen projects like this.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Well, first, As-salamu alaykum.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I've seen projects like this happen throughout the country, and I'm excited about the prospects of this particular project happening in New York.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I'm just going to say, wow, this is much needed, of course, as we look throughout.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: There's so many communities, not just Muslims, but the Jewish community, the Christian community, and the Muslim community that really need to be able to be anchored in their communities properly.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And so to have this example right here in the city is going to be really exciting.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: Thank you for that comment.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: And, Councilwoman, that question you asked, Osei asked us the same exact question.

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: He said, why are you stopping at that level?

utterances.speaker: Imam Rodney Adib Rashid
utterances.text: You know, so we had to.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right, so this panel is not excused.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony and for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council, are there any members of the public who wish to testify remotely or in person regarding this proposal?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yes, we have six people who've signed up in person to testify, and we also apparently have some members online who would like to testify.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We will first do the in-person testimony.

chapters.summary: Beatrice Sabir, a member of Masjid Khalifa for approximately 62 years, testifies emotionally in support of the project. She describes her role as dietitian and cook, notes that children from the school have become valedictorians of high schools and renowned colleges, and asks the council for support and encouragement.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Beatrice Sabir, 62-year masjid member, supports project

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The first panel we'll call is Beatrice Sabir.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Nashid.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Kareem?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Ayanna.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Muhammad.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: That's Beatrice Sabir, Nasheed Kareem, and Ayanna Muhammad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: My name is Beatrice Sabir, a member of Masjid Morsi Khalifa for, well actually my membership round and about is about 62 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: It gives me great pleasure to be able to say a few words about that building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: It's emotional.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: It's...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: It's really all that you would want it to be as a Muslim.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: We have great leadership.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: Brother Imam allow us to do what we want as long as it's right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: I've been there for many years as the dietician, the cook, and whatever it takes to nourish our children.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: Today we have the same children out of that school, Val Dictor.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: and salutatoriums of not just high school graduates, but of colleges as well, renowned colleges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: So I just want to say that whatever it takes from the council to help us to maintain or not to maintain to get our new building, that we look for your input and we look for your encouragement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Beatrice Sabir
utterances.text: And I just want to say that, brother, ma'am, continue the good work, and we are here for your support.

chapters.summary: Nasheed Kareem, a masjid member since the 1970s, testifies in support. He describes how members contributed 'nickels and dimes and sweat and tears' to the building, raised children who became professionals, and stood up against crime in the 1980s. He emphasizes the need for senior housing and the masjid's role in improving the community.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Nasheed Kareem, member since 1970s, supports project

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: As-salamu alaykum and peace be with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: You've heard that before.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: And as Muslims, that word is also has peace into it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: So we are a peaceful community that needs this project to keep the peace in the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: You know, our members, and I've been around the master, been a member there since the 70s.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: They gave their nickels and their dimes and their sweat and tears and love to that building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: You know, they raised up children there who are...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: You know, professionals and laborers in the society, you know, it's important that we have something for the future of the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: You know, everybody's invited to come to the mosque, you know, to the mosque.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: In that area,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: During the 80s, who stood up in that community when the criminals, drug dealers, you know, that set up their spaces there?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: We're not criminal fighters, but we stand up for the right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: And when we stand up like that,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: They know that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: They see that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: So the community is a better community right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: We have people moving in, different people that maybe came five, ten years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: Maybe at that time, they would never want to come around in that community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: So we...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: We need that in that area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: We need that building where the seniors, our seniors, they can have, you know, the seniors can have their place in that area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: It's very important, you know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: So I thank you for...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nasheed Kareem
utterances.text: Listening to us, and we appreciate your help today.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Ayanna Muhammad, board member and assistant treasurer of the masjid, testifies in support. She describes her experience in real estate and housing development, the pride of seeing the past generation's work, and the project's provision of dignified senior housing. She notes neighbors, local businesses, and the church across the street all support the project.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Ayanna Muhammad, board member, supports project

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: My name is Ayanna Muhammad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: I am a member of Mashimusi Khalifa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: I am also on the board of directors there as an assistant secretary, I mean assistant treasurer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: I also sit on the board of directors for the Fifth Avenue Committee on the Real Estate Committee, subcommittee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And I'm also the vice president and property manager at Park Slope North HDFC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: So I have a little bit of experience on a lot of different aspects of this project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And it's been...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: A wealth for me to be a part of this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: My mother, Yvonne Muhammad, who I believe is in queue also to testify today, was the director of community development for FAC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: With this particular project, what brings me great pride is that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: In my generation, I see all the work that the past generation has put in to bring it to this point.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And I see the deterioration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: It's not all happening physically with some of our seniors, with the building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And it's up for our generation to take it to the next place and build for the generation coming in front of us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And this project does that very well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: It's going to provide a space that is dignified for our seniors to live in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: It's also going to provide a space for our children to be educated with the values that we need to instill into them so that they can also pass the torch when it comes their turn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And so when it comes to this and speaking to not only the community, but the church across the street, neighbors, local businesses, they're all in favor because they see how much the MASHIT has put in work

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: over the years to maintain the space, maintain the community and the good faith that is in that area.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: And for nothing else, I believe that our seniors who are being displaced, they have a dignified place to live that is in their areas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: You know, we see what's happening with deed theft.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: We see what's happening with a lot of people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: Their properties are being deteriorated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: They don't know if they should sell or not or where they're going to live.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ayanna Muhammad
utterances.text: We're providing that answer for them.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you all for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This panel is now excused.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for making time to be here to testify.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The next panel is Majid Liatio.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Excuse me if I butchered your name.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Michael Shabazz.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Baz and Rima Lee.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Mashid, Michael, and Rima.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Rafer Majid Lato, financial secretary and board member, testifies in support, citing 64 years of community impact in human development, business development, and education. He notes the masjid recently helped produce two summa cum laude Spelman College graduates and that the project would bring stable housing to a changing Bedford-Stuyvesant.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Rafer Majid Lato, board member, supports project

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: My name is Rafer Majid Lato, board member, financial secretary of the community, parent of the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: And if this project is approved, it would be only the latest in a long line, 64 years of proof of impact.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: Proof of impact in the areas of human development,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: business development, and education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: When I say education, I'll plug this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: This masjid just helped produce two summa cum laude graduates at the number one HBCU in the country, Spelman College.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: That just happened May 17th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: So this is not new, but if approved, we will now step into the arena of stable housing and a changing Bedford-Stuyvesant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rafer Majid Lato
utterances.text: So thank you for your attention to the matter.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Next.

chapters.summary: Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz, associated with the masjid since the 1960s when it was Temple Number Seven, testifies in support. He notes Malcolm X's name remains on the deed and expresses hope to live in one of the new apartments upon completion.
chapters.title: Public testimony: Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz, member since 1960s, supports project

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz
utterances.text: As-salamu alaykum.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Alaykum as-salam.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz
utterances.text: And shalom to other members on the panel.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz
utterances.text: My name is Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz
utterances.text: And I must say, this project that's actually pending is very personal and dear to me because I've been actually associated

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz
utterances.text: with the masjid going back in the 60s when it was actually temple number seven.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michael Aziz Abib Shabazz
utterances.text: And I believe the imam gave some history on that.

chapters.summary: Rahima Lee, a board member, testifies in support of the project, noting that many new buildings claim affordability but are not affordable for existing neighborhood residents. She emphasizes the need for senior housing in Brooklyn, noting very little has been built in the last five years, and says the project will help seniors stay in their community.

He recounts community gatherings, his father-in-law's store on the property, and the masjid's role as a moral and spiritual anchor. He calls the project essential for community life and development for future generations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: At SUNY Downstate, which we've had a couple interactions with you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: So thank you for you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: I mean, Councilwoman Lewis, thank you for your support for our community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: And I just want to really speak to the public health impact that our masjid has really had on the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: We've hosted a number of events throughout the years in terms of wellness, in terms of screenings, making sure that our community members are screened for things like

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: colorectal cancer and prostate cancer, hypertension, and so forth, diabetes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: And it would be a shame, a tragedy, to sort of see that our institution doesn't have another 100-year legacy in this sort of impact in public health in this way.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: And I, for one,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: will be working my hardest to ensure that these sort of collaborative efforts between our medical institutions, our community-based organizations, and our faith-based institutions continue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: And we need structures to make that happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Dr. Marcus Lambert
utterances.text: So thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Marcus.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Simcha Felder thanks Chair Louis for her dedication, praises Ed Powell's presence, and reflects on the toxicity surrounding religious projects. He shares a personal story about his father's 60-year storefront church, calls the project 'holy work,' and expresses delight at the combination of senior housing and children's education in the same space, recalling his own mother's joy from contact with grandchildren.
chapters.title: CM Felder thanks chair, praises project as 'holy work' for community

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Next, Sahir.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Just a shameless plug.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I want to thank Mr.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Ed Powell, who's here from the 7th.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm the precinct in my district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I really appreciate you being here, and I know the Mashad is your place.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So I'm very happy to see you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm grateful that everybody came here to support this project.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If anyone is here to testify, I want to encourage you to please submit your testimony to land use testimony at council.nyc.gov because we're at that time.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, you have a comment?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We will now hear from Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: First of all, I wanted to thank the chair.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: For those of you that normally don't spend that much time here

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: at 250 Broadway, I just want you to, number one, to be aware that the chair puts her heart and soul into the work she does.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'm not suggesting that anyone else doesn't, but it is wonderful to see someone that's in government do the work that she does.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And of course,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Chair Ed Powell, I would just say I know for many, many years, I don't want to degrade in any way anyone else that's here, but if he was here alone

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: and he was in favor of this project,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: It should be done because anyone that knows him knows what type of person

chapters.summary: Felder continues, noting he is the last surviving member besides the chair for the entire hearing, and expresses certainty that the chair will ensure approval. He praises CM Osei as extraordinary and urges all present to do whatever they can to make the project a reality quickly, calling it a rare and fulfilling presentation.
chapters.title: CM Felder on project significance and call for swift approval

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And just finally, I just want to say, I think

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I think I'm the only survivor besides the chair that remained for the entire hearing.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And I'm happy I did because anything, unfortunately, anything that has any relationship to anything religious

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: has become toxic.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: The minute you say it, like if you said that you're just a developer, then it would be okay.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: But the minute it has anything to do with religion in any sort, suddenly it's, you know, I don't know what happens, but something...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So to listen to the testimony, my father may rest in peace,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: what I would call a storefront church for 60 years, 60 years.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I mean, and I remember growing up, no one can plunge the toilets the way I did, you know, literally.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So I just wanted to say that the work that all of you are involved in and trying to make this happen is holy work.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And it's delightful, really delightful, to...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: see that the project itself has not only the spiritual portion, but you're protecting the seniors, but not just protecting them and giving them a place to live.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: The fact that you'll have the children in the same space

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I cannot tell you that my mother, may she rest in peace, when, I'm not going to tell you all the grandchildren or great-grandchildren, but most of them,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: even if they came over, even for a few minutes, and she used to have contact with them,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: It was, I'm not going to say it extended her life because I don't know that to be true.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I will tell you it certainly gave her immense, immense satisfaction in many ways.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So I am delighted to be on the committee.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Serving with this chair, but it doesn't happen, I would just say,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: that it doesn't happen that often that we have the pleasure, really, and the honor of being, listening to a presentation

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: that actually is really

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: fulfilling.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'm not suggesting that the work that we do is not, but it's different, I must say.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So I just want to thank all of you, whoever, you know, some left.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: It's taxing, you know, to sit in one place for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: But it's clear that so many of you have been involved in this for so many years,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: and that anything that you can do to make this a reality quicker, that's what I would just say.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: The goal is...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I am certain that the chair not only will make sure it's approved, she will kill anybody who does not vote yes.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: But not, I don't mean that literally.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: She is going to be involved.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And your council member, let me tell you,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: is extraordinary, extraordinary.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So I thank you.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You're usually, you know, I really thank you for being here and presenting this to us.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right, so if there's anyone here, please submit your testimony at landus testimony at council.nyc.gov.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis closes the public hearing on LUs 108–109 regarding 1166 Bedford Avenue with no further public testimony. She thanks the public, colleagues, subcommittee, council staff, and sergeant at arms, and adjourns the meeting.
chapters.title: Hearing 4 closed, all items laid over, meeting adjourned

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Being there are no members of the public who wish to testify regarding LUs 108 and 109 relating to the 1166 Bedford Avenue mixed-use residential rezoning proposal, this public hearing is now closed and items are now laid over.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: That concludes today's business.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I would like to thank the members of the public, my colleagues, subcommittee, council, land use, and other council staff, and the sergeant at arms for participating in today's meeting.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This meeting is hereby adjourned.

summary: The Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises held public hearings on four mixed-use residential rezoning applications: 2950 West 24th Street in Coney Island (410 affordable units), 63-02 Fresh Pond Road in Ridgewood (54 units), 1160 Pugsley Avenue in the Bronx (104 affordable units with fresh food store), and 1166 Bedford Avenue in Bed-Stuy (144 senior affordable units above a reconstructed mosque).

The Coney Island application drew both support and opposition from the public, with tenants citing HPD violations and displacement concerns while 32BJ and community members supported the 100% affordable project; the other three applications received strong community support with no opposing public testimony.
All items were laid over at the close of each hearing, with no votes taken during the session.
title: Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises
